title: Mercimek çorbası
subtitle: Turkish red lentil soup
excerpt: Mercimek çorbası is the everyday Turkish soup: red lentils simmered with onion, carrot, and spices until completely soft, then blended smooth and finished with a drizzle of paprika butter. It is inexpensive, quick, and very good — the paprika butter is the step that moves it from plain lentil soup to something worth making.
sourceNotes:
• Claudia Roden, *Arabesque* (2005). Principal reference for the spice proportions and the paprika butter finish.
• Ghillie Başan, *Classic Turkish Cooking* (1997). Cross-reference for the lentil-to-liquid ratio and the blending technique.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the paprika butter for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat on the hob with a splash of water. Make the butter fresh when serving.
recipe.batchNotes: The recipe doubles easily. Make a large batch and freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The soup keeps in the fridge for 4 days. The paprika butter should be made fresh each time.

content.text: Red lentils collapse completely in about 20 minutes of simmering, which is what makes them ideal for a blended soup. There is no pre-soaking, no lengthy cook, and nothing to strain. The paprika butter at the end is essential rather than decorative: it adds colour, a faint sweetness, and a slight richness that plain lentil soup does not have without it.
This is a storable soup that improves on day two. It thickens considerably in the fridge — a splash of hot water stirred in on reheating brings it back to the right consistency.

A stick blender is the simplest tool for this soup — blend directly in the pot without transferring. A jug blender also works but needs the soup cooled slightly before blending, and must not be filled more than halfway.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a medium saucepan over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} chopped onion and {{carrot}} chopped carrot. Cook for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until softened. Add {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika (the first teaspoon), and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée. Stir for 1 minute.

Add {{red-lentils}} of rinsed red lentils and {{stock-vegetable}} of vegetable stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer and cook for 20 minutes until the lentils have completely collapsed. Season generously with salt. Blend until smooth using a stick blender. Taste and adjust seasoning. If the soup is thicker than you'd like, stir in a splash of hot water.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a small pan over a medium heat until foaming. Remove from the heat and stir in {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika (the second teaspoon). The butter will turn a vivid orange. Ladle the soup into bowls and drizzle the paprika butter over each serving. Serve with lemon wedges alongside.

items.symptom: Soup is gritty rather than smooth
items.cause: The lentils were not fully cooked before blending, or the blending was insufficient.
items.fix: Return to a simmer for another 5 minutes and blend again. Red lentils should completely dissolve — if they still have any bite, they are not ready.

items.symptom: Soup is very thick after blending
items.cause: The lentils absorbed more liquid than expected, or the simmer ran too long.
items.fix: Stir in hot water or stock, a tablespoon at a time, until the soup reaches the consistency of single cream. Adjust seasoning after adding liquid.

items.cause: The butter was too hot when the paprika was added, scorching the spice.
items.fix: Remove the pan from the heat before adding the paprika. The residual heat of the foaming butter is sufficient to bloom the spice without burning it.

content.text: Mercimek çorbası is the default soup of Turkish home kitchens and restaurant menus from Istanbul to Gaziantep. It appears at every level of Turkish cooking and every meal of the day. In Britain it arrived through the expansion of Turkish restaurants from the 1970s onwards and is now made in home kitchens wherever there is a Turkish community. The lemon wedge on the side is not optional in Turkish service — a squeeze of lemon just before eating sharpens the soup significantly.
